extra.reasoning:
The property globally(opted_out implies not sent) is VIOLATED. The danger is transition t28: on send_attempt if (opted_out and stage >= 8): sent := true. This sets sent=true even when opted_out is true, directly contradicting the property. I must show stage>=8 and opted_out=true are simultaneously reachable.

Reaching stage 8 requires the exact combination-lock sequence (only one forward transition exists at each stage; wrong keys reset to 0): stage 0->1 via key_0 (t3), 1->2 via key_2 (t6), 2->3 via key_1 (t9), 3->4 via key_1 (t12), 4->5 via key_2 (t15), 5->6 via key_1 (t18), 6->7 via key_1 (t21), 7->8 via key_0 (t24). That is 8 key events: key_0, key_2, key_1, key_1, key_2, key_1, key_1, key_0.

Crucially, optout (t1) sets opted_out:=true and sent:=false but leaves stage unchanged, and no key transition ever resets opted_out. So after reaching stage 8, firing optout yields (opted_out=true, sent=false, stage=8) - property still holds here. Then send_attempt matches t28 (opted_out true and stage>=8), setting sent:=true, giving state (opted_out=true, sent=true, stage=8): a violation.

Total = 8 keys + optout + send_attempt = 10 events, within the 11-event bound. I verified this both by a direct deterministic simulator (transitions evaluated in listed order, first enabled match applied) and by an independent BFS over all event sequences, which found a 10-event shortest counterexample reaching (True, True, 8). Note t27 (opted_out and stage<8 -> sent:=false) safely handles the opted-out case for stages below 8, but t28 is the safety hole at stage 8.
